Appearance
From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

A girl group is a popular music act featuring several female singers who generally harmonize together. Girl groups have been popular at least since the heyday of the Boswell Sisters beginning in the 1930s, but the term "girl group" also denotes the wave of American female pop singing groups who flourished in the late 1950s and early 1960s between the decline of early rock and roll and the British Invasion, many of whom were influenced by doo-wop style. This article covers only girl groups from that era and later. The term "girl group also includes Japanese and Korean idol groups. In Japan, these groups began to gain popularity in the late 1990s, while in South Korea, they gained popularity in the 2000s and early 2010s. Korean girl groups make up a significant factor in the Korean Wave.

Sales figures records in most countries are not available before the 1990s, so it is difficult to accurately determine best-selling records, either by country or worldwide. Certification levels have been used for most countries, but certification was not common until the 1970s in the US and UK, and later in other countries. In addition, in many countries certification is for shipments of a record to retailers, rather than actual sales. Complicating matters further, the changes from 2010 onwards as certifications have become based on combined sales figures and streaming instead of sales alone.

As a result, these tables should not be considered finalized of the best-selling records by girl groups in each country.

Best-selling girl groups worldwide

Best-selling girl group singles

Worldwide

It is extremely difficult to assess worldwide sales of singles, due to the lack of auditing in many countries, and that no country officially tracked sales before the 1990s. In the second edition of The Book of Golden Discs, author Joseph Murrell calculated a worldwide sales figure of 18 million singles for Baccara's "Yes Sir, I Can Boogie",[27] but this figure is disputed and has never been officially confirmed. Other claimed worldwide sales figures for singles by girl groups are shown in the table below:

Released Single Group Confirmed sales[a] Claimed sales
1996 "Wannabe" Spice Girls

Total available sales: 6.820 million
  • US: 2.910 million[28]
  • UK: 2.030 million[29]
  • JPN: 200,000[30]
  • GER: 500,000[31]
  • FRA: 750,000[32]
  • AUS: 140,000[33]
  • NLD: 50,000[34]
  • SWE: 15,000[35]
  • DEN: 45,000[36]
  • SWI: 25,000[37]
  • BEL: 25,000[38]
  • NZ: 10,000[39]
7 million[40]

Total available sales: 5.063 million

  • FRA: 750,000[43]

  • AUS: 210,000[45]

 		6 million[62]

Australia

Singles certified platinum or more by the Australian Recording Industry Association (ARIA). Only includes certifications since 1989, when ARIA took over compiling the Australian charts. From November 2014 onwards paid-for audio streams were included in the Australian singles chart and counted towards sales and certifications.

South Korea

Prior to Gaon Music Chart establishment (pre–2010)wq

The Gaon Music Chart, which was later renamed the Circle Chart, was established in January 2010. As a result, there are no available official sales data for singles released prior to that time. Nevertheless, there are unofficial claimed sales for some earlier releases.

Released Song Group Claimed sales
2009 "Gee" Girls' Generation 5,000,000[192]
2008 "Nobody" Wonder Girls 4,600,000[193]
2009 "I Don't Care" 2NE1 4,500,000[192]
Prior to certification (2010–2017)

South Korea experienced a decline in digital music sales volume which began in late 2012.[194] The price of digital downloads was greatly inflated, and as a result, no girl group songs released since 2013 have surpassed the three million sales mark.

Released Song Group Sales
2011 "Roly-Poly" T-ara 4,411,209[195]
"Don't Say Goodbye" Davichi 4,021,547[196]
"So Cool" Sistar 4,011,945[197]
"Be My Baby" Wonder Girls 3,919,684[198]
"I Am the Best" 2NE1 3,795,020[199]
"Cry Cry" T-ara 3,783,925[200]
2012 "Lovey-Dovey" 3,758,864[201]
2011 "The Boys" Girls' Generation 3,623,330[202]
"Good Bye Baby" Miss A 3,589,321[203]
2012 "Loving U" Sistar 3,438,000[204]
"Alone" 3,422,141[205]
2010 "Bad Girl Good Girl" Miss A 3,334,684[206]
2011 "Ugly" 2NE1 3,324,861[207]
2010 "Oh!" Girls' Generation 3,316,889[208]

2011 "Lonely" 2NE1 3,040,386[211]
"Starlight Moonlight" Secret 3,016,717[212]
After certification (2018–present)

The Circle Chart stopped releasing download sales in December 2017. However, the chart began implementing record certifications in April 2018, at a level of 2,500,000 sales per Platinum level.[213] Every song released since 1 January 2018 is eligible for a certification.

(Based on
certifications)

"Forever Young"
"Bad Boy" Red Velvet
"Power Up"
"Dance The Night Away" Twice
"What Is Love?"
"Yes or Yes"

Sweden

There were few certifications awarded in Sweden before 1996, so singles before this date are not represented in this list. There have been three different certification levels since 1996: from 1996 to June 2003 the gold/platinum levels for singles were 15,000/30,000, from July 2003 to September 2010 the levels were 10,000/20,000, and from October 2010 onwards, when streaming was included in the certification levels, the levels have been 20,000/40,000. The tables below reflect these changes in certification levels.

United States

This list is incomplete; you can help by adding missing items. (August 2015)

Sales figures of records in the US were not tracked accurately until May 1991, when Nielsen SoundScan started recording sales of singles and albums electronically at point of sale, rather than relying on figures provided to them by record store staff.[270] As a result, there are no reliable sales figures available before this date, and therefore it is not possible to rank the best-selling singles by girl groups in the US in sales order.

Since 2013 certifications have included legal on-demand digital streams – separate figures for the pure sales component of singles released after 2013 are not available so they cannot be compared to sales-only certifications before this date.

Certifications based on sales only

RIAA sales certifications began in the US in 1958 – there are very few records with certifications before this date. Until 1988 a million-seller received a gold certification (and a two million-seller received a platinum certification). From 1989 onwards the levels were revised so that a million-seller received a platinum certification instead, with multi-platinum awards for multiple million sales.[271][272] However, these pre- and post-1989 certification levels are not currently reflected in the RIAA database.

The following singles have been certified by RIAA as selling one million copies or more in the US.

    • "週間 デジタルシングル（単曲）ランキング – 2018年11月05日付". Oricon (in Japanese). Oricon ME inc. Archived from the original on 31 October 2018. Retrieved 31 October 2018.
    Single sales in the US: 45,000 = 33,000 ("TT") + 3,000 ("Knock Knock") + 5,000 ("Likey") + 2,000 ("Fancy") + 2,000 ("Feel Special") + 33,000 ("TT") + 3,000 ("Knock Knock") + 2,000 ("Fancy")
    • Benjamin, Jeff (3 May 2019). "TWICE's "Fancy" Revamp Leads to Biggest U.S. Sales Week for an Album Yet". Billboard. Archived from the original on 11 June 2019. Retrieved 4 May 2019.
    5,000 ("Likey")
    • Benjamin, Jeff (17 November 2017). "Twice's "Likey" Spends Second Week Atop World Digital Songs Chart". Billboard. Archived from the original on 13 June 2018. Retrieved 17 November 2017.
    2,000 ("Feel Special") Benjamin, Jeff (4 October 2019). "Twice's "Feel Special" Hits No. 1 on World Digital Song Sales & Enters Canadian Hot 100". Billboard. Archived from the original on 5 October 2019. Retrieved 6 October 2019.
  13. ^ 45,195,267 breakdown: 34.4 million claimed sales as of 17 November 2012:
    • McCurry, Justin (17 November 2012). "After Psy's Gangnam Style, here come Korea pop princesses Girls' Generation". The Guardian. Archived from the original on 17 March 2014. Retrieved 17 November 2012. ... Girls' Generation have racked up sales of well over 30m in digital singles and 4.4m albums ...
    Sales beyond 17 November 2012: Total album sales: 2,425,132 Korean studio albums: 1,065,459 = 375,886 (I Got A Boy) + 187,861 (Lion Heart) + 191,407 (Holiday Night) + 310,305 (Forever 1); Korean sales:
    • "Circle Chart's Observation Note #1 [Album Sales]". OK POP!! (오케이팝!!) on YouTube (in Korean). Circle Music Chart. 9 September 2022. Archived from the original on 18 July 2023. Retrieved 19 September 2022.{{cite web}}: CS1 maint: bot: original URL status unknown (link)

    US album sales: 234,600 = 7,000 (Square Two) + 9,100 (Kill This Love) + 117,000 (The Album) + 101,500 (Born Pink); US sales for Square Two: 7,000
    • Benjamin, Jeff (8 November 2017). "Happy Anniversary, Blackpink: A Breakdown of Their Major Chart Accomplishments After 1 Year". Billboard. Archived from the original on 6 April 2019. Retrieved 5 May 2019.
    US sales for Kill This Love: 9,100
    • McIntyre, Hugh (15 April 2019). "Blackpink Hit A New Chart Peak In The U.S. As Their 'Kill This Love' EP Debuts". Forbes. Archived from the original on 11 October 2022. Retrieved 11 October 2022.
    US sales for The Album: 117,000 = 81,000 + 19,000 + 17,000
    • Caulfield, Keith (11 October 2020). "21 Savage & Metro Boomin's 'Savage Mode II' Debuts at No. 1 on Billboard 200 Chart". Billboard. Archived from the original on 28 November 2021. Retrieved 28 November 2021.

    • Caulfield, Keith (29 October 2020). "NCT Debuts at No. 2 on Album Sales Chart as Tom Petty, BTS, Eagles & Sturgill Simpson Hit Top 10". Billboard. Archived from the original on 24 November 2021. Retrieved 24 November 2021.
    US sales for Born Pink: 101,500 = 75,500 + 26,000
    • Caulfield, Keith (25 September 2022). "Blackpink's 'Born Pink' Debuts at No. 1 on Billboard 200 Albums Chart". Billboard. Archived from the original on 25 September 2022. Retrieved 25 September 2022.
    • Caulfield, Keith (6 October 2022). "5 Seconds of Summer Score Fifth No. 1 on Billboard's Top Album Sales Chart With '5SOS5'". Billboard. Archived from the original on 7 October 2022. Retrieved 7 October 2022.
    13,786,046 single sales = 13,348,556 (South Korea) + 58,090 (Japan) + 208,400 (US) + 171,000 (Global); South Korea single sales: 13,348,556 = 605,048 ("Boombayah") + 243,508 ("Stay") + 2,500,000 ("Playing with Fire") + 2,500,000 ("As If It's Your Last") + 2,500,000 ("Whistle") + 2,500,000 ("Ddu-Du Ddu-Du") + 2,500,000 ("Forever Young"); 240,789 ("Boombayah")
    • 2016년 08월 Download Chart [August 2016 Download Chart]. Gaon Music Chart (in Korean). Korea Music Content Association. Archived from the original on 9 January 2017. Retrieved 9 January 2017.
    128,111 ("Boombayah")
    • 2016년 09월 Download Chart [September 2016 Download Chart]. Gaon Music Chart (in Korean). Korea Music Content Association. Archived from the original on 9 January 2017. Retrieved 9 January 2017.
    90,804 ("Boombayah")
    • 2016년 10월 Download Chart [October 2016 Download Chart]. Gaon Music Chart (in Korean). Korea Music Content Association. Archived from the original on 15 November 2016. Retrieved 9 January 2017.
    79,468 ("Boombayah") + 182,138 ("Stay")
    • 2016년 11월 Download Chart [November 2016 Download Chart]. Gaon Music Chart (in Korean). Korea Music Content Association. Archived from the original on 17 June 2018. Retrieved 9 January 2017.
    65,876 ("Boombayah") + 61,370 ("Stay")
    • 2016년 12월 Download Chart [December 2016 Download Chart]. Gaon Music Chart (in Korean). Korea Music Content Association. Archived from the original on 17 June 2018. Retrieved 9 January 2017.
    2,500,000 ("Playing with Fire") + 2,500,000 ("As If It's Your Last")
    • C., Daniel (26 February 2019). GAON′s 관찰노트 [GAON′s Observation Notes]. Circle Chart (in Korean). Korea Music Content Association. Archived from the original on 30 December 2023. Retrieved 30 December 2023.
    2,500,000 ("Whistle")
    • C., Daniel (28 November 2019). GAON′s 관찰노트 [GAON′s Observation Notes]. Circle Chart (in Korean). Korea Music Content Association. Archived from the original on 30 December 2023. Retrieved 30 December 2023.
    2,500,000 ("Ddu-Du Ddu-Du") + 2,500,000 ("Forever Young")
    • "Certification – Download". Circle Chart (in Korean). Korea Music Content Association. Archived from the original on 30 December 2023. Retrieved 30 December 2023.
    Japan single sales: 58,090 = 29,388 ("Ddu-Du Ddu-Du") + 8,604 ("Kill This Love") + 11,106 ("How You Like That") + 7,438 ("Pink Venom") + 1,554 ("Shut Down"); Japan physical sales for "Ddu-Du Ddu-Du": 29,388 = 24,385 + 2,878 + 1,160 + 965
    • 週間 デジタルアルバムランキング 2018年09月03日付 [Weekly Single Ranking 3 September 2018]. Oricon News (in Japanese). p. 1. Archived from the original on 29 August 2018. Retrieved 29 August 2018.
    • 週間 デジタルアルバムランキング 2018年09月10日付 [Weekly Single Ranking 10 September 2018]. Oricon News (in Japanese). p. 3. Archived from the original on 5 September 2018. Retrieved 5 September 2018.
    • 週間 デジタルアルバムランキング 2018年09月17日付 [Weekly Single Ranking 17 September 2018]. Oricon News (in Japanese). p. 5. Archived from the original on 12 September 2018. Retrieved 12 September 2018.
    • 週間 デジタルアルバムランキング 2018年09月24日付 [Weekly Single Ranking 24 September 2018]. Oricon News (in Japanese). p. 5. Archived from the original on 19 September 2018. Retrieved 19 September 2018.
    Japan digital sales for "Kill This Love": 8,604 = 4,372 + 4,232
    • 週間 デジタルシングル(単曲)ランキング 2019年04月15日付 [Weekly Digital Single Ranking 15 April 2019]. Oricon News (in Japanese). p. 2. Archived from the original on 11 April 2019. Retrieved 11 April 2019.
    • 週間 デジタルシングル(単曲)ランキング 2019年04月22日付 [Weekly Digital Single Ranking 22 April 2019]. Oricon News (in Japanese). p. 2. Archived from the original on 17 April 2019. Retrieved 17 April 2019.
    Japan digital sales for "How You Like That": 11,106 = 5,506 + 5,600
    • "週間 デジタルシングル(単曲)ランキング 2020年07月06日付" [Weekly Digital Single Ranking 6 July 2020]. Oricon News (in Japanese). p. 2. Archived from the original on 1 July 2020. Retrieved 1 July 2020.
    • "週間 デジタルシングル(単曲)ランキング 2020年07月13日付" [Weekly Digital Single Ranking 13 July 2020]. Oricon News (in Japanese). p. 2. Archived from the original on 8 July 2020. Retrieved 8 July 2020.
    Japan digital sales for "Pink Venom": 7,438 = 3,236 + 2,674 + 1,528
    • "週間 デジタルシングル(単曲)ランキング 2022年08月29日付" [Weekly Digital Single Ranking 29 August 2022]. Oricon News. p. 2. Archived from the original on 24 August 2022. Retrieved 15 September 2022.
    • "週間 デジタルシングル(単曲)ランキング 2022年09月05日付" [Weekly Digital Single Ranking 5 September 2022]. Oricon News. p. 3. Archived from the original on 31 August 2022. Retrieved 15 September 2022.
    • "週間 デジタルシングル(単曲)ランキング 2022年09月12日付" [Weekly Digital Single Ranking 12 September 2022]. Oricon News. p. 5. Archived from the original on 7 September 2022. Retrieved 15 September 2022.
    Japan digital sales for "Shut Down": 1,554
    • "週間 デジタルシングル（単曲）ランキング 2022年10月03日付" [Weekly Digital Single Ranking 3 October 2022]. Oricon News. p. 5. Archived from the original on 28 September 2022. Retrieved 28 September 2022.
    US single sales: 208,400 = 113,000 + 5,000 + 4,000 + 7,000 + 11,000 + 7,000 + 7,000 + 16,400 + 23,000 + 11,000 + 4,000; 113,000 (Square Two songs) + 5,000 ("As If It's Your Last")
    • Benjamin, Jeff (8 November 2017). "Happy Anniversary, Blackpink: A Breakdown of Their Major Chart Accomplishments After 1 Year". Billboard. Archived from the original on 6 April 2019. Retrieved 5 May 2019.
    4,000 ("As If It's Your Last")
    • Benjamin, Jeff (30 June 2017). "Blackpink Score Third No. 1 on World Digital Song Sales Chart, Lead K-Pop Females". Billboard. Archived from the original on 30 June 2017. Retrieved 30 June 2017.
    7,000 ("Ddu-Du Ddu-Du")
    • Zellner, Xander (25 June 2018). "BLACKPINK Makes K-Pop History on Hot 100, Billboard 200 & More With 'DDU-DU DDU-DU'". Billboard. Archived from the original on 4 October 2019. Retrieved 26 June 2018.
    11,000 ("Kiss and Make Up")
    • Benjamin, Jeff (29 October 2018). "BLACKPINK Score Second Hot 100 Entry Thanks to 'Kiss and Make Up' With Dua Lipa". Billboard. Archived from the original on 4 October 2019. Retrieved 30 October 2018.
    7,000 ("Kill This Love")
    • Zellner, Xander (15 April 2019). "Blackpink's 'Kill This Love' Makes K-Pop History on Hot 100 & Billboard 200 Charts". Billboard. Archived from the original on 25 April 2019. Retrieved 5 May 2019.
    7,000 ("Sour Candy")
    • Murray, Gordan (4 June 2020). "Lady Gaga's Collabs With Ariana Grande & BLACKPINK Shine on Hot Dance/Electronic Songs Chart". Billboard. Archived from the original on 11 July 2020. Retrieved 10 June 2020.
    16,400 ("How You Like That")
    • McIntyre, Hugh (6 July 2020). "Blackpink's New Single Debuts As The Second-Bestselling Song In America Behind Kanye West". Forbes. Archived from the original on 6 July 2020. Retrieved 6 July 2020.
    23,000 ("Ice Cream")
    • Trust, Gary (8 September 2020). "Blackpink Hits New Hot 100 High With Debut of Selena Gomez Collab 'Ice Cream'". Billboard. Archived from the original on 10 September 2020. Retrieved 18 September 2020.
    11,000 ("Pink Venom")
    • Rutherford, Kevin (1 September 2022). "BLACKPINK Achieves This First-Time Feat on Streaming Songs Chart With 'Pink Venom'". Billboard. Archived from the original on 1 September 2022. Retrieved 1 September 2022.
    4,000 ("Shut Down")
    • Rutherford, Kevin (29 September 2022). "BLACKPINK Nabs Third Streaming Songs Chart Top 10 With 'Shut Down'". Billboard. Archived from the original on 29 September 2022. Retrieved 29 September 2022.
    Global single sales: 171,000 = 70,000 (The Album songs) + 46,000 ("Pink Venom") + 55,000 (Born Pink songs); The Album songs: 70,000
    • Frankenberg, Eric (14 October 2020). "Here's Where Every Song on Blackpink's New Album Debuts on the Billboard Global Charts". Billboard. Archived from the original on 1 July 2022. Retrieved 1 July 2022.
    "Pink Venom": 46,000 = 36,000 + 7,000 + 3,000
    • Trust, Gary (29 August 2022). "Blackpink's 'Pink Venom' Blasts in at No. 1 on Billboard Global Charts". Billboard. Archived from the original on 29 August 2022. Retrieved 29 August 2022.
    • Trust, Gary (6 September 2022). "Blackpink's 'Pink Venom' Tops Billboard Global Charts For Second Week". Billboard. Archived from the original on 6 September 2022. Retrieved 6 September 2022.
    • Trust, Gary (12 September 2022). "Harry Styles' 'As It Was,' Blackpink's 'Pink Venom' Rule Billboard Global Charts". Billboard. Archived from the original on 12 September 2022. Retrieved 12 September 2022.
    Born Pink songs: 55,000
